Discipline-specific conceptual knowledge: Broad knowledge of human genetics & molecular biology
Deep knowledge of the molecular mechanisms of human genetic disorders
Broad knowledge of genetic and genomic analytical and testing methodologies

Deep knowledge of the applications, strengths, validation methods, and limitations of important genetic and genomic testing methodologies in research and/or clinical diagnostic settings (e.g. NGS testing)
Deep knowledge of the fundamental and logistical differences between clinical and research genetic testing
Broad knowledge of clinical trial coordination, set-up, and execution
Broad knowledge of fundamental statistical concepts applied to genetics
Deep knowledge of statistical tests used to analyze genomic data (e.g. NGS, GWAS, single-cell sequencing)
Broad knowledge of one or more programming languages, including literacy in interpreting code written by others
Deep knowledge of one or more programming languages to process and analyze ‘omics-level data
Deep knowledge of genomic data file types and formats
Critical evaluation of scientific and medical literature
Broad knowledge of the clinical genetics and genomics landscape in Ontario/Canada (e.g., services available, access to testing, funding agencies, key payers and payment models, major stakeholders)
Broad-based knowledge of the funding landscape for genomics research in Canada and internationally, including funding pools for commercialization, industry, and startup companies
Discipline-specific practical skills: Interpretation and use of major genetic and genomic databases (e.g., OMIM, gnomAD, ClinVar, COSMIC)
Reading, writing, and manipulating code in one or more programming languages (e.g., R, Python)
‘Omics-relevant statistical analysis
Distributed cluster / cloud computing, & working with big data (i.e., running pipelines using UNIX / command line)
Validating informatics software
Bioinformatics pipeline creation / implementation for ‘omics analysis (e.g., WES, WGS, RNASeq, Bisulfite-Seq)
Interpreting results / data from different types of genetics tests (e.g., panels, whole genome sequencing, RNA sequencing)
Interpreting results / data in either a germline or somatic context
Data visualization and figure creation
Data visualization for assessment of mapping and variant call quality (e.g., using a genome browser)
Application of ACMG and other professional guidelines in germline variant analysis (e.g., VCEP guidelines)
Application of AMP / ASCO and other professional guidelines in somatic / cancer variant analysis (e.g., CAP guidelines)
Application of ClinGen and other professional guidelines for gene curation
Assessment of case and functional studies for variant analysis
Assessment of in silico prediction for variant analysis
Technical laboratory skills in genetics / genomics methodologies
Following protocols, standard operating procedures (SOPs) and guidelines
Laboratory safety
Critical evaluation of data generation and analysis pipelines
Tolerance / acceptance of uncertainty and ambiguity
Tolerance for repetitive / inconclusive, and for troubleshooting inconclusive / failed work
Establishing frameworks to collect, analyze, and interpret data and information from a variety of sources
Communication skills: Keeping a lab book & documenting lab work
Maintaining good records and documentation of dry lab work / clinical work (e.g., spreadsheets, reports)
Generating thorough documentation, commenting, and version control on computer code
Writing clear and accurate research articles
Writing comprehensive and well researched literature reviews
Writing grant proposals
Writing new SOPs / laboratory guidelines
Writing for medical professionals
Writing for non-scientists
Writing genetic test reports
Writing clear, tactful, and professional emails
Generating and using slides effectively (e.g., PowerPoint, Keynote)
Speaking clearly, effectively, and persuasively
Presenting to / teaching scientists or medical professionals
Presenting to / teaching non-scientists or non-medical professionals
Presenting to / teaching the public or non-technical audiences
Effectively and succinctly synthesizing / summarizing complex information to generate insights
Communicating why individual scientific studies or findings / results fit into a broader scientific question or program
Chairing / coordinating / hosting successful meetings
Creating and implementing a communications plan; understanding different communication techniques
Creating visual communications (e.g., posters, infographics, figures)
Leveraging social media platforms to communicate science
Training and mentoring individuals
Effective verbal and written communication with peers and colleagues
Seeking advice and asking for feedback / clarification from advisors and mentors when needed
Navigating difficult conversations

Complying with rules, guidelines, policies, laws, and regulations
Upholding commitments and meeting deadlines
Working independently, with initiative, and in a self-motivated fashion

Managing / leading a team, and / or working with direct reports
Planning, organizing, establishing priorities, and time management
Adapting to changing goals, projects, and circumstances at work
Creativity / innovative and strategic thinking
Problem solving and critical thinking
Working under pressure / managing tight deadlines
Resilience, self-awareness, and mindfulness of personal wellness and well-being
Building and maintaining positive relationships with colleagues, and an encouraging, supportive, and psychologically safe workplace culture
Managing conflict and conflict resolution, building consensus, compromising, and exercising tact and diplomacy in the workplace
Listening / being attentive
Providing constructive feedback
Receiving and incorporating constructive feedback
Contributing to ongoing workplace professional development (e.g., member of committees / professional societies)
Contributing to and incorporating awareness and adoption of inclusion, diversity, equity, and accessibility issues and guidelines in the workplace
Working with and adapting to the needs of patients / clients / stakeholders / community members from diverse and varied populations
Identifying career options and setting career goals
Creating and maintaining a professional network (e.g., meeting people, maintaining connections)
Arranging and performing informational interviews
Preparing application materials (e.g., cover letters & resumes)
Interviewing (both behavioral and skill-based)

Marketing oneself effectively when seeking and applying for job opportunities (e.g., building a strong professional reputation, pitching oneself to potential employers, standing out from other applicants)
Ethical and responsible conduct: Understanding data ownership, sovereignty & sharing issues
Understanding privacy and disclosure issues in the context of medical genetics
Understanding informed consent in the context of genetic testing
Understanding the impact of genetic testing technologies on patients and the healthcare system
Understanding responsible conduct in human research (e.g., clinical trials, obtaining ethics approval)

Identifying and responding to ethical and legal issues in a professional and clinical environment with moral courage (i.e., when it is not easy to do so; e.g., when it involves a peer or a supervisor)
Demonstrating responsible writing practices (i.e., referencing appropriately)
Demonstrating accountability for one’s actions, and a dedication to acting in accordance with the highest standards of professionalism

Assessing business trends, strategies, and competitive activity; entrepreneurial ideas

Career Values: Help Society: contribute to the betterment of the world
Help Others: be involved with directly helping individuals / small groups
People Contact: have day-to-day contact with clients / colleagues
Teamwork: work in collaboration with others, as a part of a team
Friendships: develop close personal relationships with people at work
Congenial atmosphere: work with friendly colleagues
Competition: engage in activities that test my abilities / achievements against others’ abilities / achievements
Make decisions: have the authority to decide courses of action, policies, etc.
Fast pace: work in a busy atmosphere with frequent deadlines
Supervision: be directly responsible for the work done by others
Influence people: be in a position to change the attitudes or opinions of other people
Work alone: work on projects by myself, with little contact with others
Independence: work with little direction from others
Intellectual challenge: perform work that is intellectually stimulating
Work on the frontiers of knowledge: engage in the pursuit of knowledge or generating new ideas
Expert status: be acknowledged as an expert in a given field
Creativity: originate and develop new ideas
Aesthetics: appreciate the beauty of the things and ideas that I work with
Predictability: have job duties that are similar day-to-day
Variety: have job duties that change frequently
Job security: be assured of keeping my job and salary
Benefits available: have health, retirement, tuition reimbursements, etc.
Recognition: be recognized or appreciated for the quality of my work
Risk taking: have work duties that involve trying new things, despite the chance that negative outcomes could result
Earning potential: have a salary which allows me to purchase essentials as well as some luxuries of life
Location: live in a place which is conducive to my lifestyle
Physically challenging: have a job that requires high physical demands
Not physically challenging: have a job that does not require high physical demands
Flexible schedule: have some choice over the hours or days that I work
Status and prestige: work in a position or organization which carries respect with my friends, family, or colleagues
Professional development: have a job with opportunities for growth or promotion
Job tranquility: work in a low-pressure environment
Work/life balance: balance time spent at work and time spent doing other activities
Family friendly: have a job with policies supportive of families (e.g. daycare, flexible work schedules, etc.)
Exercise competence: take advantage of my strongest talents and skills on a regular basis
Learn new things: be challenged to learn new skills or knowledge on a regular basis
High demand: develop a desirable knowledge base or skillset to facilitate finding my next job
